title: How parts respond differently to different therapeutic modalities
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your parts don't all want the same thing 

 I remember sitting in a therapist's office years ago, frustrated and confused. I'd been doing talk therapy for...
htmlContent:
Your parts don't all want the same thing

I remember sitting in a therapist's office years ago, frustrated and confused. I'd been doing talk therapy for months, and while I felt smarter about my childhood, I didn't feel any different in my body. My anxiety was still there. My nightmares were still there. The part of me that believed I was broken was still there, loud as ever.

Here's the thing. I thought I was failing at therapy. I thought maybe I just wasn't trying hard enough, or that my trauma was somehow "too much" for therapy to touch.

But what I didn't understand back then is that different parts of me needed different things. And different therapeutic modalities speak to different parts.

This is something I've come to see clearly over years of my own recovery and through studying the work of people like Janina Fisher, who specializes in structural dissociation, and Richard Schwartz, who created Internal Family Systems. These researchers understand something that most of us don't learn anywhere: your psyche is not a single, unified thing. It's a system of parts. And those parts don't all respond to the same approach.

Think about it. The part of you that goes numb when someone raises their voice might need something completely different from the part of you that's always scanning for danger. And the part that holds the memory of what happened to you? That part might need something else entirely.

When I finally understood this, everything shifted. I stopped blaming myself for not getting better faster. I started matching the modality to the part.

The manager parts and their love for structure

Let's talk about what Pete Walker calls the "manager" parts. These are the parts of you that try to keep things under control. They're the ones that make sure you're on time, that you don't rock the boat, that you appear put-together even when you're falling apart inside.

These parts love structure. They love frameworks. They love knowing what to expect.

For manager parts, cognitive approaches can work really well. CBT, when done carefully and not in a dismissive way, can give these parts a sense of mastery. They get to learn skills. They get to identify distorted thoughts and challenge them. They get to feel like they're doing something.

But here's the catch. Manager parts often use intellectual understanding as a way to avoid feeling. I know this because I've done it. I've read 50 books on trauma while my body was still holding patterns from 30 years ago. The manager part loves that. It feels safe. It feels productive.

I think about exile parts a lot. In IFS language, exiles are the young, wounded parts that carry the pain, the shame, the memories that the protector parts work so hard to keep locked away. They're the parts we're most afraid of. Because if we let them speak, if we let them be seen, we're afraid the pain will swallow us whole.

But here's what Richard Schwartz found in decades of clinical work. When exiles are finally witnessed - when someone (even your own Self) turns toward them with compassion instead of away from them with fear - they don't destroy you. They soften. They release the burdens they've been carrying. Sometimes for decades.

You don't have to do this alone. And you don't have to do it all at once. But I want you to know that those parts of you that feel too much, that carry too much, that seem too broken to touch - they're not too anything. They're just waiting. Waiting for someone to say "I see you. I'm not leaving. You don't have to carry this alone anymore." And sometimes, that someone can be you.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Walker's work gives manager parts a map. It explains why you are the way you are. It validates the strategies you developed to survive. And that validation is often exactly what these parts need to start trusting the process.

But if you only work with the manager parts, you might find yourself stuck. You'll understand everything intellectually. You'll have all the vocabulary. But you still won't feel safe in your own skin.

That's because the parts that hold the actual trauma don't respond to intellectual understanding. They respond to something else entirely.

The exiled parts and the body's language

Here's where things get interesting. The parts that hold the painful memories, the shame, the terror, the grief - these are what Schwartz calls "exiles." They're the parts you've been trying to keep hidden, even from yourself.

These parts don't speak in words. They speak in sensations. In images. In impulses. In the way your stomach clenches or your shoulders tighten or your breath gets shallow.

Talk therapy can completely miss these parts. I've sat in chairs and talked about my childhood for hours, and the exile parts just sat there, unheard, unseen, still holding the same pain they've held for decades.

This is where body-based modalities come in. Somatic Experiencing, created by Peter Levine. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, developed by Pat Ogden. These approaches speak the language that exile parts understand.

Stay with me here. When a part holds trauma, it's frozen in time. It doesn't need you to analyze what happened. It needs you to notice what's happening right now in your body. It needs you to slow down and track the sensations. It needs you to stay present with the fear or the numbness or the shaking without trying to make it go away.

I remember working with a part that held a memory I couldn't even verbalize. My throat would close up. My hands would go cold. In talk therapy, I would have just talked around it for weeks. But in a somatic session, I just noticed the throat closure. I stayed with it. I let my body complete the response it had been frozen in for so long.

That part didn't need me to understand the story. It needed me to feel the sensation and let it move through.

Wild, right? The parts that hold the deepest wounds don't care about your insights. They care about being felt.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It helps you understand that every part, even the ones that seem destructive, is trying to protect you somehow.

The firefighter parts and their urgent needs

Then there are the firefighter parts. These are the ones that kick in when the exiles get triggered. They're the emergency response team. And they don't care about subtlety.

Firefighter parts are the ones that make you binge eat, drink too much, dissociate, lash out in anger, or shut down completely. They're trying to put out the emotional fire by any means necessary.

These parts don't respond well to traditional therapy approaches. Not at first. They're too reactive. Too urgent. Telling a firefighter part to "breathe through it" is like telling someone whose house is on fire to just relax.

What firefighter parts need is containment. They need structure that's firm but compassionate. DBT, developed by Marsha Linehan, can be incredibly helpful here. It gives these parts skills for distress tolerance. It helps them find alternative ways to manage overwhelming emotions.

But here's what I've learned. Firefighter parts also need to feel appreciated. They need to know that you understand they're trying to help. They're not the enemy. They're the part of you that's been working overtime to keep you from drowning in pain.

Here's something that changed everything for me. The idea that I'm not one unified self. That the part of me that wants connection and the part that pushes everyone away - they're both me. They're both trying to help. They just have very different strategies.

Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model gave language to something I'd felt my whole life but couldn't name. Those contradictions inside you? The way you want intimacy but sabotage every relationship? The way you crave rest but can't stop working? Those aren't character flaws. Those are parts. And every single one of them has a reason for existing.

Think about the part of you that's reading this right now. Is it curious? Skeptical? Hopeful? Afraid? Whatever it is, can you acknowledge it without trying to change it? That's the beginning of parts work. Not fixing. Not silencing. Just acknowledging. "I see you. I hear you. You make sense." Wild how powerful those words can be when directed inward.

When I started thanking my firefighter parts instead of fighting them, something shifted. The binge eating didn't stop overnight. But the shame around it started to ease. And that shame was making everything worse.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps firefighter parts understand why they developed such extreme strategies in the first place.

Matching the modality to the part

So how do you figure out which part needs what? Honestly, it takes practice. And it takes patience. But here are some things I've noticed.

If a part makes you feel smarter, more in control, more capable, it's probably a manager part. These parts will thrive with psychoeducation, cognitive work, and structured approaches. They need to feel like they have a handle on things before they'll let you go deeper.

If a part makes you feel flooded, overwhelmed, or numb, you're likely touching an exile. These parts need body-based work. They need slowness. They need you to track sensations rather than stories. They need you to stay present without trying to fix anything.

If a part makes you want to escape, distract, or self-destruct, that's a firefighter. These parts need immediate coping skills and containment. They need to know there are other ways to survive intense feelings.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did any part of you tense up? Did another part feel relieved to finally be seen? That's your system responding. That's the information you need.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It helps you track which parts show up and what they need.

This is not about finding the one right modality

I want to be really direct about something. The wellness industry loves to sell you the idea that there's one perfect approach. One magic modality. One thing that will fix everything.

That's not how parts work. Your system is complex. It developed complex strategies to survive. It needs complex support to heal.

Some weeks, I need more structure. I need to read, to understand, to feel competent. That's my manager parts asking for what they need.

Other weeks, I need to lie on the floor and track the sensations in my chest. I need to let my body shake or cry or just be still. That's my exile parts asking to be felt.

And some days, I need to just survive. I need to use every coping skill I have and not judge myself for it. That's my firefighter parts asking for appreciation and support.

Right? It's not about choosing one modality and sticking with it forever. It's about learning to listen to your parts and giving them what they actually need, not what you think they should need.

This is why I get frustrated when people say "CBT doesn't work for trauma" or "somatic work is the only way." Both statements miss the point. Different parts respond to different things. The skill is in learning to discern which part is present and what it needs right now.

I'm still learning this. I still get it wrong sometimes. I still try to intellectualize when I need to feel, or try to feel when I need structure. That's okay. That's part of the process.

What matters is that you keep asking. Keep listening. Keep letting your parts show you what they need.

They've been trying to tell you for a long time. It's time to start listening.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
